title: CPU, RAM & Storage
subtitle: Compare RAM vs. ROM vs. HDD/SSD — Know Your Memory!
overview: Students explore the three memory heroes of every computer — RAM, ROM, and Storage (HDD/SSD). Using real-world analogies like a chef's kitchen, students understand where data lives, how fast it moves, and why computers need different types of memory. Includes a hands-on comparison activity and deep dive into how the CPU talks to each one.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — RAM sticks, HDD opened, SSD chip, CPU close-up, whiteboard kitchen analogy diagram.

keyTopics: CPU (Central Processing Unit) — The Chef! Processes all instructions | Measured in GHz (billions of operations per second)
CPU Cores — One chef vs. many chefs | Dual-core, Quad-core, Octa-core = more tasks at once
RAM (Random Access Memory) — Kitchen Counter: fast, temporary, VOLATILE | Data vanishes when power off
RAM Speed & Size — Measured in GB and MHz | More RAM = more apps open at once | DDR4, DDR5
ROM (Read-Only Memory) — Recipe Book: permanent, NON-VOLATILE | Stores BIOS/firmware | Cannot be easily changed
Cache Memory — The Chef's pocket: tiny, ultra-fast memory inside CPU | L1, L2, L3 Cache
HDD (Hard Disk Drive) — Spinning Pantry: magnetic platters spinning at 5400–7200 RPM | Cheap, slow, large
SSD (Solid State Drive) — Flash Pantry: no moving parts, uses NAND flash chips | Fast, silent, expensive per GB
NVMe vs SATA SSD — NVMe (M.2 slot) is 5× faster than SATA SSD | NVMe = Formula 1, SATA SSD = regular car
Volatile vs Non-Volatile Memory — Volatile (RAM) loses data on power off | Non-Volatile (ROM, HDD, SSD) keeps data
Memory Hierarchy Pyramid — Cache (fastest) → RAM → SSD → HDD (largest, slowest)
How CPU Uses Memory — Fetch from storage → Load into RAM → Process in CPU cache → Output result

analogies.explanation: The chef (CPU) does all the cooking (processing). A faster chef = faster meals. More chefs (cores) = more dishes at once!
The counter holds ingredients the chef is currently using. Bigger counter = more ingredients out at once. But clear the counter when done — it's temporary!
The recipe book has fixed instructions. You can read it but not easily change it. It survives even if the kitchen loses power!
Tiny pocket holding the most-used spices RIGHT next to the chef. Ultra-fast access! But very small — only the most important things fit.
A big pantry with shelves that spin to find ingredients. Lots of storage but slow — you have to wait for the shelf to spin to the right spot!
A modern pantry where everything is instantly accessible — no spinning, no waiting. Faster than HDD but smaller and costs more per GB.

comparisonTable.title: RAM vs ROM vs HDD vs SSD — Quick Comparison

activity.title: Memory Comparison Chart + Kitchen Analogy Role Play
parts: Printed comparison chart worksheet (RAM vs ROM vs HDD vs SSD)
Colored markers (4 colors — one per memory type)
Memory Hierarchy pyramid printout (blank, to be filled)
Real components to pass around (if available): RAM stick, SSD, HDD
Speed race cards (for class quiz game)
steps: Show the Kitchen Analogy slide — assign roles: students act as Chef (CPU), Counter (RAM), Recipe Book (ROM), Pantry (HDD/SSD)
Role play: Chef asks Counter for ingredients → Counter asks Pantry if empty → Pantry finds and delivers
Fill in the comparison chart worksheet — students color-code each memory type
Pyramid Activity: arrange memory types from fastest → slowest on the blank pyramid printout
Speed Race Game: show a task (e.g. 'Load a game'), students decide which memory type is used first, second, third
Real hardware round: pass around a RAM stick, SSD, and HDD — identify connectors, size, weight
Discussion: Why does your phone freeze when RAM is full? (RAM = counter is too small for all the dishes!)
Challenge: If your laptop is slow, what upgrade helps most — more RAM or bigger HDD? (Answer: more RAM or SSD!)

options: Central Power Unit
Central Processing Unit
Computer Program Utility
Core Processing Uplink
quiz.explanation: CPU = Central Processing Unit — the brain of the computer! It processes all instructions, calculations, and logic operations.

quiz.q: In the Kitchen Analogy, what does RAM represent?
options: The chef cooking the food
The recipe book with fixed instructions
The kitchen counter — fast, temporary workspace
The pantry storing all ingredients
quiz.explanation: RAM = Kitchen Counter! It holds data the CPU is actively working on. It's fast and close to the CPU — but cleared when power is off!

quiz.q: What happens to RAM data when you turn the computer OFF?
options: It saves automatically to the hard drive
It stays in RAM permanently
It is lost completely — RAM is volatile
It moves to ROM for safekeeping
quiz.explanation: RAM is VOLATILE — data vanishes the moment power is cut! That's why you save your work to storage (HDD/SSD) before shutting down.

quiz.q: What does ROM stand for and what is it used for?
options: Random Output Memory — for printing
Read-Only Memory — stores permanent firmware like BIOS
Rapid Online Memory — for internet data
Reusable Optical Memory — for DVDs
quiz.explanation: ROM = Read-Only Memory! It stores permanent instructions like the BIOS that start your computer. You can read it but not easily change it.

options: Volatile — data is lost when power is off
Non-volatile — data is kept even without power
It depends on the computer brand
Neither — ROM has no memory
quiz.explanation: ROM is NON-VOLATILE — it keeps its data even when the computer is turned off. That's why BIOS settings survive a power cut!

quiz.q: What makes an HDD (Hard Disk Drive) slow compared to an SSD?
options: HDD uses less electricity
HDD has spinning magnetic platters that must physically rotate to read data
HDD stores less data
HDD is connected by slower cables
quiz.explanation: HDD = Spinning Pantry! Magnetic platters spin at 5400–7200 RPM and a read head must physically move to find data — that mechanical movement takes time!

quiz.q: What is the main advantage of an SSD over an HDD?
options: SSD stores more data for less money
SSD has spinning platters for reliability
SSD has no moving parts — it is much faster and silent
SSD uses magnetic storage like a cassette tape
quiz.explanation: SSD = Smart Pantry! No spinning parts means instant data access. SSDs are 5–10× faster than HDDs, completely silent, and more shock-resistant.

options: A type of external USB storage
Tiny ultra-fast memory built directly inside the CPU for most-used data
A backup copy of the RAM
The memory used by the graphics card only
quiz.explanation: Cache = Chef's pocket! L1, L2, L3 cache sits inside the CPU and holds the most frequently used data for lightning-fast access — faster than even RAM!

quiz.q: Which memory type is at the TOP of the memory hierarchy pyramid (fastest)?
options: HDD
RAM
SSD
CPU Cache (L1)
quiz.explanation: CPU Cache (L1) is at the top — the fastest memory! Pyramid goes: Cache → RAM → SSD → HDD. Faster = smaller and more expensive per GB.

quiz.q: Your computer has 4GB of RAM and you open too many apps. What happens?
options: The CPU speeds up automatically
The computer uses the HDD/SSD as slow 'virtual RAM' and slows down
ROM takes over and helps RAM
The apps delete themselves
quiz.explanation: When RAM is full, the OS uses the HDD/SSD as virtual memory (page file/swap). This is MUCH slower — like putting things on the floor because the counter is full!

quiz.q: What does GHz measure in a CPU?
options: Gigabytes of storage
Gigahertz — the number of processing cycles per second (speed)
Gigawatts of power used
Gigapixels of screen resolution
quiz.explanation: GHz = Gigahertz = billions of cycles per second. A 3.5 GHz CPU does 3.5 BILLION operations every second. More GHz generally means faster processing!

options: The power socket of the CPU
An independent processing unit inside the CPU — more cores = more parallel tasks
The cooling fan attached to the CPU
The cache memory layer
quiz.explanation: Core = Individual Chef! A quad-core CPU has 4 independent processors inside. More cores = more tasks handled simultaneously — great for gaming and video editing!

quiz.q: What type of storage does an SSD use?
options: Spinning magnetic platters
Optical laser discs
NAND Flash memory chips (no moving parts)
Magnetic tape reels
quiz.explanation: SSD uses NAND Flash chips — the same technology as a USB pen drive but much faster. No spinning, no moving parts, no noise!

quiz.q: NVMe SSD vs SATA SSD — which is faster?
options: SATA SSD is faster because it is older technology
They are exactly the same speed
NVMe SSD is faster — it uses the PCIe slot and is up to 5× quicker than SATA SSD
NVMe is slower but cheaper
quiz.explanation: NVMe = Formula 1 car! It plugs into the PCIe/M.2 slot and reaches speeds of 3500 MB/s vs SATA SSD's 550 MB/s. NVMe wins by a mile!

quiz.q: If you want to make a slow old laptop faster WITHOUT buying a new one, what is the BEST single upgrade?
options: Replace the screen
Add more USB ports
Replace the HDD with an SSD
Change the keyboard
quiz.explanation: Swapping HDD → SSD is the single biggest speed boost for an old laptop! Boot times drop from 2 minutes to 15 seconds. It feels like a brand new machine!
